---
title: "Needlepod Rush (Juncus scirpoides)"
slug: "juncus-scirpoides"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622601355/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Needlepod Rush"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Juncaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Juncaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622601355/medium.jpg" alt="Needlepod Rush" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/marykeim">
marykeim</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341946653">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Juncaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=164280)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida.

## Natural history notes

A grass-like plant characterized by its slender, upright stems and small, clustered seed heads that appear along the sides of the stems. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as marshes, ditches, and pond margins. It flowers and sets seed primarily in the warmer months and provides cover and structure for small wetland organisms.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/164280-Juncus-scirpoides)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/1017)
